title: When Reality Blinked
content:
Year 2026

In a quiet bedroom, Alex didn't notice any of it.

He was sitting cross-legged on his bed, controller in hand, eyes glued to his monitor. Detroit: Become Human glowed across the screen—his tenth walkthrough.

"Okay, Markus, don't mess this up," Alex muttered.

His glasses lay forgotten on the desk beside him. He didn't need them that badly—at least, that's what he told himself.

The air in his room warped.

For half a second, the walls shimmered. Time folded inward, silently. Something invisible slipped through, scanning the room—not with eyes, but with intent.

The Reality Entity had arrived.

It sensed a human presence. A stable timeline. A hiding place.

Its gaze settled on the glasses.

Carbon frame. Minor scratches. Constant physical contact with the host's brain.

Optimal vessel detected.

The entity merged.

Nothing exploded. No lights flashed. The glasses remained exactly where they were.

History, however, had just lost track of itself.

____________________________________________

The next morning, Alex slipped the glasses onto his face and headed to school.

He felt... normal.

Maybe a little tired. Maybe a little sharper than usual.

On the way to class, he heard shouting.

A smaller kid was pressed against the lockers, three students laughing as one shoved him harder.

"Hey," Alex said, stepping in without thinking. "Back off."

The bullies scoffed—until Alex didn't flinch.

It ended quickly. Too quickly.

They left, muttering excuses.

Alex frowned. That was easier than it should've been.

He shrugged it off and walked into class.

____________________________________________

At his desk, his best friend Ryan leaned over. "You hear the news?"

Alex sighed. "If it's about fights again, yeah. Whole school's gone crazy."

Ryan nodded grimly. "Because Ethan isn't here."

Ethan.

The name carried weight.

He was their other best friend—and the unofficial enforcer of peace. Seniors listened to him. Juniors feared him. Teachers tolerated him.

And now he was suspended.

Without Ethan, bullying wasn't whispered anymore. It was loud.

____________________________________________

Lunch hour was chaos.

In the cafeteria, Alex froze.

Ten students surrounded one boy near the tables. Shoving. Laughing. Phones out.

Alex clenched his fists.

He wasn't a hero. He didn't jump into every fight.

But one thought burned through his mind.

If Ethan were here...

He'd already be running.

Ryan sighed beside him. "Even though his suspension's over, he can't come back yet. New principal hasn't joined. Needs their signature."

"So... impossible?" Alex asked quietly.

"For a few days, yeah."

Alex looked down.

I wish he was here right now.

____________________________________________

A voice echoed behind them.

"That enough?"

Silence slammed into the cafeteria.

The bullies turned.

Color drained from their faces.

Standing there—hands in pockets, calm eyes scanning the scene—was Ethan.

Chairs scraped. Someone dropped a phone.

The crowd parted.

Alex's heart pounded. "Ethan...?"

Ethan smirked. "Miss me?"

____________________________________________

None of them knew what had changed.

That, early that morning, a principal appointment had been fast-tracked. The suspension papers were signed sooner. Reality hadn't broken.

It had simply been edited.

And resting quietly on Alex's nose, the Reality Entity waited.

Learning.

Listening.

Because this was only the first wish.
